text: I think the MachE deserves a spot on this list.  While it doesn't have the highest range or fastest charging, it is incredibly refined. Oh, and it is hands-down the best looking Electric crossover, and maybe even the best looking crossover of any type.   I regularly have people stop and ask me if it is a jaguar or some other exotic. The Lease deals on it are excellent as well.

text: Too bad Hyundai can't get the batteries right. In 2020, we bought a Kona EV—great little car until the battery quit. Dealership bought back the car, and we switched to a Model 3. 60,000 miles later, zero problems. Last spring, I test drove an Ionic 5 and loved it. Was getting ready to pull the trigger when once again battery failure stories began to emerge. At the last minute, I bought a 2026 Model Y instead and haven't looked back. We take a 2K mile road trip every year, and the Model Y is flawless. Plus, you still don't see many other brands out charging on the long stretches of road in California. Definitely didn't see any Ionic 5s. In short, from my experiences and for my needs, the Ionic still isn't ready for prime time.

text:
I'm a year and a half in, I've not yet gone outside the range of my EV. I'm not concerned about when I do either, I generally stop every 2 hours on longer trips otherwise I'd be sitting in a wet seat 😂

Also my insurance was the same price as my previous 2l diesel. 

I'd still happily own and weekend a classic, but day to day I'll never go back to ICE; BEV is just so much more convenient (if home charging is possible), plus costing me less than 1.5p/mile.

WLTP isn't a range prediction, it's a standardises test of efficiency, if you drive two cars in the same way with the same WLTP, you'll likely get the same efficiency (whatever that is - it won't be the figure stated), it's just a way to compare cars against each other. Think of it this way - comparing two electric kettles under lab conditions. One boils a litre of water in 90s the other in 75s. Your time will depend on line voltage, starting water temperature and altitude, but the one that boiled in 75s will be quicker than the one that boiled after 90s.

Insurance for my Nissan Leaf here in Australia is the same as my previous petrol car and I just use the granny charger at home over night using your standard home socket and there are some charging sites that are still offering free charging or free 7kwh and then charge only a little bit more than what im paying at home so its at least 50% cheaper than petrol here in Australia.

text: My test for getting an EV was what I called the Old Trafford test. Could it get me from my home in York to Old Trafford for football and cricket and back without charging. I've had it nearly three years now and I've had no issues. It's easier in fact because I can often top up at the metrolink park and ride stops and not have to worry about setting off with a full charge. If my niece didn't insist on stopping at Starbucks whenever she comes it'd be a straight run -depending on the M62 of course.

text: If you are a US resident and happen to be watching this, the situation here is similar to the UK, although charging costs are different. The statement that "only people who have the ability to charge at home should even consider an EV" is just as true in the US as in the UK. Our public charging network is still way behind the level it will need to be if EV's are ever to see wide acceptance. Even worse than the inadequate number of public DC fast charging stations is their cost: on average, these stations are charging 50¢/kW-hr, compared to 12¢/kW-hr for overnight home charging. Do the math: public EV charging on average costs four times more than home charging. So if you live in an apartment complex, or a house that doesn't have a garage or private carport, you won't be able to charge an EV at home, and that covers about 33% (1/3) of all American households.
clean_text: if you are a us resident and happen to be watching this, the situation here is similar to the uk, although charging costs are different. the statement that "only people who have the ability to charge at home should even consider an ev" is just as true in the us as in the uk. our public charging network is still way behind the level it will need to be if ev's are ever to see wide acceptance. even worse than the inadequate number of public dc fast charging stations is their cost: on average, these stations are charging 50¢/kw-hr, compared to 12¢/kw-hr for overnight home charging. do the math: public ev charging on average costs four times more than home charging. so if you live in an apartment complex, or a house that doesn't have a garage or private carport, you won't be able to charge an ev at home, and that covers about 33% (1/3) of all american households.

text:
I compare EV home charging with using a dishwasher. Yes, it's faster to wash the dishes by hand, but while the dishwasher is  washing the dishes, I can be doing something different, anything it doesn't matter. Home charging the EV isn't any different, as I'm not holding the plug in the socket, as depicted in a Toyota Self Charging Hybrid advert showed.

Longer journeys over the battery range is no problem either. Tomorrow on a 300 mile trip, I'll leave early to miss the traffic, then have breakfast 1/2 way, and give the car a 20 minute top up while I'm going something else. 
It's not that difficult to live with an EV, at least when a little bit of forward planning is involved.

text:
There is a certain anti EV youtuber who has just put out a video about the TVR Sagaris - I assume because its almost the ultimate petrolhead car, the polar opposite of any EV. As far from an EV as you could possibly get. But...

I own a TVR Sagaris, and for that matter a Lotus Exige - and they are both brilliant, fast, noisy and make me smile. They are the perfect sunny Sunday toys

However I also own a Ford Explorer EV and god is it good. Its dull as dishwater to drive, utterly soulless, but for moving the kids, dog, doing the weekly shop its perfection. Charging issues - well every anti EV person 'get over yourself' just been to the South of France and back and zero issues charging with zero range anxiety. Oh and its does 4 mile per kilowatt. Each kilowatt on EON Next tariff costs me 6.7p so that 67p for 40 miles. i.e equivalent about 67p per gallon!

If you can charge from home its a no brainer, you must be mad not to go EV - chargers everywhere. but most EV's these days do 250 miles plus so how many trips will you realistically need to charge doing so.  Doesn't mean however if its sunny on Sunday morning I wont take the Lotus or TVR out though instead :)

text: Here's what bothers me the most about electric cars -- the 1,200 pound battery. Not that it degrades and loses capacity over time, but rather what is done with it when it eventually dies and is no longer useful. They could be re-cycled, and attempts have and are being made to do so, but for the most part (as of now), the process is too costly and time consuming to be economically viable. Which means that most batteries (98.3% to be exact) are currently disposed of in land fills. That's 1,200 pounds of lithium, nickel, cobalt, manganese, aluminum, copper, high grade graphite and silicon. 1,200 pounds. Per car. Thrown away.

text:
One issue that is coming down the road (for the other half of the 'most people most of the time') is that, just like petrol forecourts, there are safety requirements for bringing the high power electrical connections to the vehicles, where part of the electrical safety is to ensure that 'leakages' go down the designated drains, and don't leak into adjacent 'drains' (that's fault currents into earth connections). If you and you neighbour happen to be connected to different supplies (different earths), then current regulations require a 2.5m separation (between all electrical bits, inc the cars!). It also applies to you and the street lights (etc). 
The situation is being discussed. It may result in a similar, careful, de-scoping of those rules, just as the Heat Pump 1m rule has just been de-scoped.

text:
It's important to distinguish between a charger (fast, DC) and a charge point (slow, AC, what you have at home).  To make the EV roll out work we all need to do the majority of our charging at charge points.  Ideally using intelligent scheduling so that all the EVs can help to balance the grid.

Obviously it's easiest if you can have a charge point at home but there do also exist areas with plentiful supplies of public charge points suitable for overnight charging.  The small town which I live in has 24.  Where you live clearly doesn't have them and that needs fixing.  What we need is these to be available everywhere.

text:
I remember when I first started looking into EVs and came across channels like Bjørn Nyland's channel, his mantra on longer trips was ABC - always be charging. And I think it's still a useful one (well, maybe less necessary in Norway where the charging network has reached similar coverage as petrol stations). If you stop for whatever reason and there is a charging option, just plug it in - it's not necessary, but it gives you more flexibility on where to stop and charge later in the trip. 

Having owned an EV for seven years now (a 2018 Model S 75D), I agree with most of the points you make. The servicing cost has been minimal, even in a car that some consumer organisations (eg Which?) like to take a dump on for reliability, has been minimal. Changing air filters, servicing the air conditioning, swapping wind screen wipers, that's about it. In addition, a problem with the boot locking mechanism was fixed under warranty, and we paid to install the hardware for the CCS adapter as it wasn't around when we first got the car. The tires have typically lasted 30-40k miles before needing a change, which is hardly excessive wear and tear. And the battery degradation has been pretty modest - a 4.2% loss of range over 7 years and around 70k miles, almost all of it during the first 30k after which the degradation curve has been flat as a pancake.

So yeah, when eventually we replace it the replacement will definitely be another EV - might even try a BYD next.

More generally, I still enjoy the channel - keep up the good work!
clean_text: i remember when i first started looking into evs and came across channels like bjørn nyland's channel, his mantra on longer trips was abc - always be charging. and i think it's still a useful one (well, maybe less necessary in norway where the charging network has reached similar coverage as petrol stations). if you stop for whatever reason and there is a charging option, just plug it in - it's not necessary, but it gives you more flexibility on where to stop and charge later in the trip. having owned an ev for seven years now (a 2018 model s 75d), i agree with most of the points you make. the servicing cost has been minimal, even in a car that some consumer organisations (eg which?) like to take a dump on for reliability, has been minimal. changing air filters, servicing the air conditioning, swapping wind screen wipers, that's about it. in addition, a problem with the boot locking mechanism was fixed under warranty, and we paid to install the hardware for the ccs adapter as it wasn't around when we first got the car. the tires have typically lasted 30-40k miles before needing a change, which is hardly excessive wear and tear. and the battery degradation has been pretty modest - a 4.2% loss of range over 7 years and around 70k miles, almost all of it during the first 30k after which the degradation curve has been flat as a pancake. so yeah, when eventually we replace it the replacement will definitely be another ev - might even try a byd next. more generally, i still enjoy the channel - keep up the good work!

text:
Drive Chevy Bolt EV here in US. Just did an 1100 mile trip over two driving days in it. Definitely takes more time than an ICE, though honestly the stops aren't bad, and I can sit in air-conditioned car and listen to music or read while I'm waiting. On our second day we found a pretty good Indian restaurant at the charger stop for lunch, which was fun.

Of late the Tesla superchargers have opened up to my vehicle (with an adapter) which makes planning these trips much easier. At home we have a nice Level 2 charger which makes refueling basically effortless.

WRT time spent charging at home, some years back I had both an ICE and an old Leaf that we charged in the garage. I honestly spent far more time (and money) refueling the ICE than I did on the Leaf, even though we drove the Leaf more than the ICE. This even though the Leaf only charged at 3.3 KW and had less than 100 miles range.

text: I always recommend getting an EV with double the range you need. That way, you've got plenty of reserve, for blasting the heat in the winter, and the extra rolling resistance of cold tires tromping through snow, which can cut your range by as much as 40%. On top of that, you have enough reserve, to compensate for the inevitable degradation of battery capacity, allowing you to keep the car for decades, while maintaining adequate range. It's well worth the extra money, to get the biggest battery available in your particular model.

text:
I also have 10- years of EV ownership experience and i also have always been able to charge at home, and i also agree that the charging network could be better!  BUT i also can see that the overal charging network has got a LOT LOT better.  I personally now have something like 15 or more DC fast chargers (=>50kW) within about 5ml of my house, whereas in 2015 there were none (in fact, none within 50ml of my house!).

With regards to Total Cost of Ownership, then my experience is that whilst "fueling" an EV might be about as expensive or possible slightly more than fuelling an equivalent ICE if you can't charge at home (if you can charge at home, the cost is so low as to be irrelevant for most!) when you look at TCO, the EV has a massive win once you start looking at multiple years of ownership. For example servicing. If you wanted, you could either not service your EV at all, or "DIY service" it and there is really nothing that would cause any concern, but even with main dealer servicing, the lack of servicing rerquirements is a large cost saving. My diesel van recently needed a cambelt as it reached 50kml, that cost over £700, so that single routine service that  you really really woudn't want to not do, cost about the same as ALL the servicing done on our 'lecy cars in 10 years!

And again, even if the servicing cost the same as for an ICE model, i'm driving a car that is much faster, mure nicer to drive, more reliable, safer, more practical (bigger inside) and simply much nicer day to day (cabin preheating, not having to wait for engine to warm up each day before thrashing it etc)

I'd say for the vast majority of people, for the cost, even if that might in-extremis be the same or slightly more than for an equivalent ICE model, you are getting a better product and better value for money!

This is finally starting to be reflected in social media where the always idiotic "what about the cobalt mines" and "you'll need a million pound battery when the car is 3 years old" comments are now by far in the minority, rather than being the majority. The fact is, as more people own and drive BEVs on a daily basis, we will honestly wonder what the fuss was all about (see incandescent lightbulbs etc etc)

text: Looked at buying an EV about a year ago but decided on an ICE.  Why?  I do have to drive long distances on motorways regularly and the range is always poor while cold weather range is worse.  I did several calculations and using an EV would  make these journeys significantly longer.  Battery degradation with age (2% pa) would make this worse.  Secondly, my estimate was that the initial cost was about 15% - 20% higher for the EV, depending on dealer discount, and the depreciation was greater, not always true but it was in my case.  This increased cost was not fully offset by the reduction in fuel expenses (I can charge at home).  I appreciate the point about short day to day journeys, so it is probable that I may buy an EV as a local runabout when I am too old for longer journeys then I would get a secondhand one (with a battery condition certificate) and take advantage of the depressed market..

I settled for one with 200 miles (real world) range because I figured so long as I had the range to do my work I could take the time and money hit of charging to get home. As for battery repair, first it is the exception rather than the rule and second check out the cost of having a wet belt replaced in your ICE car! Factor in around £1000 every 80k or 90k miles on top of normal servicing costs.

They are heavy. The technology can be annoying and a challenge. For example, a new EV driver has no one pedal driving experience, and manufacturers make several versions of it. text: I agree. I've driven electric since 2015 and I certainly didn't do it to save money or for the environment. Living in Australia, most of our power was generated and lot still is powered by coal. I bought one because it was nicer, quicker and more convenient to drive. We had solar panels and a driveway, something that makes an EV hard to ignore, the ability to precondition your cabin and battery before you set off is almost essential. Over the past 10 years, fast charging infrastructure has significantly improved, as has the reduction in coal power stations. Power companies now offer very good EV plans if you have solar panels and an EV. We live in Brisbane and travel up Hervey Bay or south to Coffs Harbour with ease, although most of my work travel is metropolitan. None of my cars have had “annual servicing”, just a few doorhandles, airsuspension modules and an MCU upgrade. Oh and tyres. Because my EVs like tyres. I say my EVs, because my wife's Model 3 Performance is still on her first set. The best part of an EV, is that in the hypothesis a motor goes, it is replaced and the problem is fixed, unlike an ICE vehicle, where even a minor fault, like a busted water pump can completely shut an engine down. You can replace an engine, but you are bound to be left with gremlins. In an EV, particularly in Teslas, a replacement fixes the problem and is generally an improvement.

text:
The charger situation is very very variable. For the first 2 years I didn't use my home charger because ChargePlace Scotland units were free. Then as EVs took off you started to see queues and now they have tariffs so I moved to using my home charger.

In the last three years the numerous CPS rapid chargers have been joined by an mfg ev hub (12 250 kW units), a forev hub (4 250 kW units), BP pulse 4 350 kW units, pogo are building another 6 250 kW units and that's within 3 miles of my house. And if you go up to 10 miles away there's 2 more mfg hubs, 2 new tesla superchargers an Arnold Clark hub etc. So the reality here isn't the same as down south that you're seeing. 

Otherwise a Cracking rebuttal of all the usual anti ev nonsense. Oh and it's not just Vauxhall, the Škoda Elroq is also the same price as the Škoda Karoq (the similar ice car in the Škoda range)

Both have saved us over 5000€ in gas during the last year. We  about 100km each roundtrip everyday to commute to work, that's almost 50000kms a year between the two of them. Gas would be well over 3000L of gas with current prices here would mean well over 5500€. Our night charging power plan allows us to charge at 0.003€/kWh which makes it 10% the cost per km of gas.

It's just magic.
Some tips:
- Range doesn't mean as much as you think it does. A 60KWh battery is enough for 99.9% of case usage.
- Chaging at home is critical, as well as a night EV plan and a timer on your car or charger (Tesla allows you to set schedules in the app).
- Consider used market. People are panicky about used EVs, we had two, both worked way better than gas cars of equivalent money. Just test it and check overall condion and battery range. Not much else can go catastrophically wrong.
Our Renault Zoe cost us 4500€ which has already paid itself over in running costs savings. The Tesla will take longer (but it's a way nicer car too!).

text:
My three nuggets (sorry if you did cover Amazon delivery mid way through) 

How much range in a charge? with a 7kW home charger gives you 'cheap rate' about 130 miles of range overnight, totalling roughly 850 miles a week. In summer, that's closer to 180 miles daily. I know you can charge longer, nut most chase the cheap overnight rate.

Ive heard a few times you can't run two EV's. Er Yes you can - It comes down to common sense. Unless both cars are doing say 150-mile daily trips, a standard home charger with alternate-day charging works fine. We have had two for years now and cant remember a single day where both wanted a charge urgently enough to trigger a mid charge swap over.

Final one is don't fall for the "long range EV" trap which I see so much with new people thinking they need the longest range and others telling them they do (Forums or mainstream Reviewers). That extra £5,000+ is a choice, but for just a three or four long trips a year, it might only save you 30 minutes of charging time per trip. Over four years, that minor time-saving could effectively cost you around £250 per 30-minute stop - chill out and have the alternative of a quick sandwich and coffee and your overall cost of ownership will be lower as you wont be lugging the bigger battery around impacting you normal mpk

I've got a Nissan LEAF and a petrol car.  The LEAF only has a realistic range of 100 miles/160km.  Despite the small range I have only put petrol in the other car twice in 2 Years, possibly 4 times in 3 years (I forget).

I didn't buy one to save the planet.  I bought one to improve my life.  Next year I will trade the LEAF for a used Kia E-Niro.  Then I can sell the petrol car because the E-Niro will give me the range I need for all my journeys.

text:
The benefits are undeniable.
My only worry about EVs applies to all new cars in general.
- Too much complexity
- Too hard to repair and maintain by yourself
- Active measures by manufacturers to cut out 3rd party support

This is where a lot older cars still on the road has an advantage for the few still willing to maintain and repair cars themselves.
I can replace pads, discs and even the engine for next to nothing myself compared to similar repairs done by a mechanic with an newer car (including EVs).

Theres no technical or engineering reason that this advantage exists, only financial ones. Newer cars are designed this way on purpose.

The warranty and reliability on EVs are undeniably superior to newer cars in a 10 year perspective, but what about 20? At a certain point in the lifespan of a car, parts start to fail more frequently.
At this point these parts can generally be replaced and maintained almost indefinitely on older cars that have managed to survive to this day thanks to this design principle in mind.
Newer cars (including EVs) are not designed this way.

The "manufacturers lifespan" is longer yes, but when that ends and parts start failing, they are close to impossible to repair.

I'm know I'm in a minority here, but I would much rather have a warranty of 3 years on a car that's simple, easy to maintain and easy to repair forever, than an 8 year warranty on a car that's a nightmare to maintain and repair with hideously expensive parts and no 3rd party support.

I cant weld in new structural elements on a Tesla when inevitably get eaten away by salted roads.
I can't replace the battery myself when a couple of cells start to fail.
I can't upgrade or update the electronics when the software inevitably starts to slow or support gets cut by the manufacturer.
When the manufacturer decide the car is dead, it's dead, and there's very little you can do about it.

These cars are too heavy to climb up a kerb or to go on speed bumps without thumping and thudding. Too heavy to do corners properly. Software rules everything so the battery management system drops mileage in  hot and cold weather so in effect you are losing money which you have paid in charging. Software glitches can only be found by authorised dealers, and they often don't find the glitch either knowingly or unknowingly. Public chargers are far too expensive now and there is no free road tax. Installing a charger at home is a nightmare the checklist of minomum amperes, fuse board, surge protector,  space and the costs can easily be upwards of 1200 pounds. Never charge with a granny charger this is a fire hazard in your home. Many chargers go bad either within 3 years warranty or outside 3 years warranty for no apparent reason. Even Wi-Fi connection of charger cannot be done by you if you change your broadband provider, you have to call someone. The depreciation is very high, no one wants your 5 to 6-year-old EV. If it's 8-year-old then it has zero value due to no battery warranty. I have driven 8 year old ice cars comfortably before. When you leave your car in dark secluded public chargers then you wish you had a petrol car. The video is completely wrong in saying car insurance is 20% higher, in reality it's 100% higher. I would pay around £450 for a petrol car but ev is minimum 940 car insurance. A 16E insurance group versus 47A, e means exceed a means acceptable risk to insurers. Plus minor dents cannot be repaired like I have previously done, an EV must go for specialist repairs or be completely written off due to the risk on the battery. The risk, if you ignore it can cause fires as your EV is parked due to minor unseen fractures in the battery. I am regretting buying EV and would recommend that you dont.

text:
I had a Nissan Leaf a few years ago and long journey's were a nightmare. I'm sure with longer range ev's, long journeys are much easier, but for me, the nagging wife complaining about stopping or the hassle involved with waiting to charge etc. is too much to bare. Until you can find a solution to the nagging wife, I'll stick to my diesel long range vehicle 😂
My issue with the EV community is the lack understanding that EV's are not great for everyone. Certain lifestyles and driving routines are not suitable for electric vehicles. In my opinion, electric vehicles should make up part of the motor industries fuel source, with a mixture of Petrol, Diesel, Hybrid and Electric.  I dont see why that isn't a great idea. The issue comes with the forced nature at which we're being pushed into only being able to have EV's. If you allowed people to make informed decisions, then over time the market would find its equilibrium, and we'd see a mixture of all fuel types. But while the government are hell bent on forcing people into EV's, there will always be pushback.

text:
For me an EV beats out an ICE vehicle in 4 major areas: Cost, convenience, comfort and climate change.

1) Cost: Despite an initial higher purchase cost (unless you buy used) and added insurance and registration costs (which vary greatly depending on where you are and may actually be cheaper for the EV), the running costs (especially when charging at home from my own solar power) and thus overall ownership costs are so insanely cheaper that I would be mad to revert to gasoline. 
2) Convenience. Charging at home and never having to go to a gas station again is a massive plus. For the 1% of journeys when I might actually have to use a public charger, I'll take the hit of a 20 minute delay. In winter I can preheat my EV while plugged in, if range is likely to be an issue for that day.
3) Comfort. I didn't have a gasoline car for the noise, vibration and smell of a gasoline engine, I had it because there wasn't a choice. An EV does away with almost all the engine noise vibration and smell of petrol. I'm left with just road and wind noise, which are unavoidable.
4) Climate. I accept the scientific consensus that fossil fuel use is adding to global warming, and we need as a planet to reduce our carbon dioxide emissions to try to mitigate the damage our species is doing to the planet. Solar panels and an EV are my tiny contribution to this effort. I also benefit greatly for the reason above, so it's not exactly a noble sacrifice. 
My kids and those around me have to suck in less exhaust pollution, which will reduce health problems in later life.

text:
OK, now you have truly demonstratede that you don't know what you are talking about. Cobalt is NOT what fuels an EV fire. It is the oxygen rich ELECTROLYTE that boils off and causes the thermal runaway. It has NOTHING to do with the CObalt.

The problem with the cobalt, which obviously you dont know, is that it is extremely toxic. How toxic? Two firefighters in VIctoria, Australia, about 2 years ago were exposed to a Lithium battery fire with a battery containing cobalt. Note that it wasnt an EV, it was something else.

Despite the fact that they were wearing full PPE, these two firefighters sustained PERMANENT DISABILITIES from absording cobalt compounds into their skins. The nature of their disabilites is not known. Their union representative said that they wish to have medical privacy on that matter, and that is respected. Think about that. Permantently disable through absorbing toxic "smoke" despite wearing full PPE.

Indeed LFP batteries do not contain Cobalt. LFP batteries are indeed LESS PRONE to thermal runaway. But they still do if they are heated hot enough for the electrolyte to decompose; and yes, that requires a higher temperature than for LNC batteries. For example, here in my home state in Queensland, the first big battery we ever had was an LFP one. Despite the best BMS, water cooling, and 24/7 monitioring, it still caught on fire, it still went into thermal runaway, and all the fireies could do is keep the crowd away until the fire burnt itself out. So yeah, it still happens.

There are still some cars that use the cobalt (LNC) batteries. Because LNC batteries, despite their dangers, are a higher performance battery. Greater energy holding capacity, and greater discharge capability. Musk said that by 2023 he would not be using LNC batteries at all. When asked in 2023, he said he was about 60% there. WHat the situation is now with Tesla and LNC I dont know and dont really care.

text: I'm a foreigner living in Chongqing, China for over a decade, a mega city of 22+ million people. Here, 50%, if not more, of the cars are now EV, 90% of the buses and taxis are now also EV, aside from all the Chinese brands (XPENG, LI, XIAOMI, NIO, ZEEKR, BYD, GEELY, LEAPMOTOR, AITO, AVATR, CHANGAN, ...) there are an immense amount of Teslas as well. The Model 3 and Y are around the same price as the Chinese EV's to keep up with the competition. Some drive an electric Mercedes or BMW but you don't see them often. I drive a Model Y myself here. I believe that one of the main reasons that EV's are so successful here is because they built a whole network of charging stations that works with every province's state grid, each highway gas station has at least 12+ super charging stations (and growing), each parking lot, outside or under a shopping mall will have charging stations, and then even in the middle of nowhere in small countryside villages in the mountains, you will find charging stations. On top of that electricity here is very cheap, I spent around 15 dollar a month (!!!) instead of 300+ dollar a month compared to my previous gas driven GLK before buying a Tesla. I hope that back at home in Europe governments will start building a network of charging stations with cheaper electricity to make EV's more viable.

text: I tend to pay close attention to companies that sit upstream in emerging industries because supply chain positioning often matters more than the finished product branding. Talga Group has caught my attention for that reason. The company is focused on developing graphite anode materials used inside lithium ion batteries, which are a core component of electric vehicle technology. As more countries move toward electrification and localized battery production, upstream materials suppliers can become strategically important pieces of that ecosystem. When I compare Talga to other advanced materials and graphene related names in the market, it appears aligned with where long term demand may develop. It is still a growth stage company and there are execution and financing risks involved, but from a structural supply chain perspective, it is one I believe is worth researching further. This is simply my personal opinion and not financial advice.

text:
I am Chinese, and I also have an EV (pure electric) car, which is a 1.7-ton SUV with a 60-kWh battery. It only takes 6.6 seconds to accelerate from 0 to 100 km/h, and I have driven 8,000 km. Charging speed: It only takes 23 minutes to charge from 20% to 80%. It only takes 55 minutes to charge from 15% to 100%. My car has a relatively backward charging technology. Now high-end batteries only take 23 minutes to charge 100 degrees. Yes, you heard it right.
In fact, I used to believe in internal combustion engine cars like many Chinese (especially the old predecessors), and I didn't fall in love with EV cars until I bought an EV car.
The day before yesterday was Saturday, and I took my girlfriend to the beach to camp. To be precise, who was in the car, because EV cars can keep the air conditioner on and the windows closed, so there is no need to worry about carbon monoxide poisoning. Yes, the two of us slept from 2 in the evening until 10:30 the next morning, and only consumed 4.2 kWh of electricity. It is summer now, and the temperature outside is 32℃. I set the air conditioner in the car to 22℃, automatic gear.

The battery capacity of my EV car is only 60 kWh, which is a very low-end EV car. But it has a high-speed range of 363km and a constant speed of 120km/h.
The urban range (commonly known as low speed: ≤80km/h) is 503km. Both of these ranges are with the air conditioner on: 22℃, automatic gear.
As I said just now, my car is a very low-end EV car, and there are many high-end EV cars in China. They have a very long high-speed range, such as BYD's Tang L, with laser radar and high-level intelligent driving system, equipped with a 100-kWh battery, a high-speed constant speed of 120km/h, and can run 580km (in the southern plains). The low-speed range can reach 750km. This is a 2.6-ton SUV, and it only takes 6.9s to accelerate from 0 to 100km/h.

In China, the charging fee ranges from 0.28-1.5 yuan/kWh. China implements off-peak electricity consumption, so from 0:00 p.m. to 8:00 a.m., it only costs 0.28 yuan/kWh (0.039 U.S. dollars/kWh), and even in the afternoon, charging is only 0.84 yuan/kWh (0.117 U.S. dollars/kWh).
In other words, even if calculated at 0.117 U.S. dollars/kWh, if I drive 3,000 km per month, the cost of driving this car is only: 50 U.S. dollars/month,
and if I drive my gasoline car, the monthly cost is: 268.78 U.S. dollars/month, obviously EV pure electric car is more economical and affordable.
Some people may ask: After driving 300,000 kilometers, your car battery is scrapped. Is it expensive to replace the battery?
In fact, the price of battery packs in China is now: about 400 yuan/kWh (55.7 U.S. dollars/kWh). To be precise, this is the price of last year, and the price this year will be lower. Because my battery is lithium iron phosphate battery, which is a relatively mature technology, the production cost will only get lower and lower. Back to the point, even if I replace the entire battery, it will cost $3,342, which already includes the labor cost. But under normal circumstances, I will not replace the battery. I will scrap the car and give it to the manufacturer, and then successfully buy a new car. Because in China, electric vehicles are updated every 6-8 months. I think after a few years, I will like other electric vehicles and will not want to drive the current car anymore. Just like my other internal combustion engine cars, it is quietly parked in the garage.

text:
Just back from my son's middle school (public Middle School here in N. Scottsdale AZ) class trip to China for 11 days (Beijing, Xi'an, Chengdu, Shanghai).  What I saw were more cars than anywhere else (largest auto market in the world and bigger than U.S. and Europe markets combined).  More variety too, with dozens of brands I've never seen in the U.S.  Even saw a Mercedes-Maybach S-Class parked in front of one of the restaurants we ate at. 

New infrastructure everywhere you look, every city we saw had dozens of new skyscrapers under construction on top of the 100's already built.  New freeways going up, new high-speed rail being built on top of the 41,000 km (25,000 miles) of high-speed rail already built.  Took 2 bullet trains (from Beijing to Xi'an and from Xi'an to Chengdu that were travelling up to 307 km/hr. (191 mph).  At one point we travelled over 40 miles under a mountain range in a tunnel on the way to Chengdu.  I doubt we have any tunnels that long in the U.S. Also saw several nuclear power plants with the giant cooling towers while on the bullet trains (usually in rural areas but one was in a small city).  All 3 airports looked modern, new and spacious and better than any airport I've been to here (and I've been to dozens over the years).  Their version of TSA is much faster and smoother than here and no taking off you belt/shoes or even emptying pockets.  Cities were also very clean and not what I has imagined them to be prior to the trip.  In Philippines (was there in 2024) looked less clean and developed in comparison except for the BGC area of Manilla.  

Also, around their cars in Beijing (with 22 million people), about 75% of the cars there were EV's but they have a law that M-F, 20% of gas powered cars must remain parked each day (depends on what their license plate ends in 0-9 - i.e. on Mondays blue plates ending in 0 or 5 can't drive).  So punishes the gas drivers even more as they have to use an EV or public transit.  This rule wasn't in place in Xi'an or Chengdu both have more than 10 million population.  


Not everything about the country was great however, you see camera's everywhere (supposedly to prevent crime but there is no privacy).  Still have squatty potties everywhere for women in our group and rarely any tissue paper.  Also, no napkins supplied at restaurants unless asked for and rarely anything to drink (their tradition is to eat first and only drink tea/water after the meal) which seemed very odd.  Also, traffic was horrendous, traffic jams every hour of the day especially in Beijing and Xi'an.  Plus had my text messages with family in U.S. cut off at one point saying I violated some decency law or some such.  But using a VPN and another channel was able to get back in touch.  Still felt way too invasive and had to watch what I said for the rest of the trip.  Also, no handicap access on sidewalks (didn't see a single person out of probably 100k people that was in a wheelchair).  Another complaint would be how prevalent smoking is everywhere (indoors and outdoors).  That terrible smell of cigarette smoke was hard to deal with and often caused us to move to an area without that smell.  I also talked to some locals about issues and a few opened up that there are issues with alcoholism and drugs as well as homelessness (hard to believe when they have an estimated 90 million unused housing units built).  So, the country still has much room for improvement but so do we.

i.e. on mondays blue plates ending in 0 or 5 can't drive). so punishes the gas drivers even more as they have to use an ev or public transit. this rule wasn't in place in xi'an or chengdu both have more than 10 million population. not everything about the country was great however, you see camera's everywhere (supposedly to prevent crime but there is no privacy). still have squatty potties everywhere for women in our group and rarely any tissue paper. also, no napkins supplied at restaurants unless asked for and rarely anything to drink (their tradition is to eat first and only drink tea/water after the meal) which seemed very odd. also, traffic was horrendous, traffic jams every hour of the day especially in beijing and xi'an. plus had my text messages with family in u.s. cut off at one point saying i violated some decency law or some such. but using a vpn and another channel was able to get back in touch. still felt way too invasive and had to watch what i said for the rest of the trip. also, no handicap access on sidewalks (didn't see a single person out of probably 100k people that was in a wheelchair). another complaint would be how prevalent smoking is everywhere (indoors and outdoors). that terrible smell of cigarette smoke was hard to deal with and often caused us to move to an area without that smell. i also talked to some locals about issues and a few opened up that there are issues with alcoholism and drugs as well as homelessness (hard to believe when they have an estimated 90 million unused housing units built). so, the country still has much room for improvement but so do we.

text:
Not all green plate in China are EV. Green plate can be "EV" or "hybird vehicle"
In most case, you can tell the difference by looking at the thrid letter.

Take the vehicle plate (粵ADF2271) at 0:08 as an example.
粵 means Guangdong province.
A stands for provincial capital city of Guangzhou.
D means its all electirc. (where F means hybird).

In conclusion, for green plate, in most case, third letter can tell if its EV or hybird.
But sometimes in cities which ran out of combination, the third letter can be different.

text: Was in Italy, Germany, Switzerland and Czech for a few months. The entire time I was there, I saw only one Chinese EV, a BYD. I approached the owner in the coffeeshop to ask him his thoughts on his car. He shook his head and said "bad decision". I asked my close friends who lives in Texas and Chicago. The one in Texas said he'd never seen a single Chinese EV on the road. The friend from Chicago said he doesn't recall seeing any. I found all of this to be surprising as I live in Bangkok and there are plenty of Chinese EVs around - but sales is surely declining and people are reconsidering going back to legacy car makers. All this "Chinese EV taking over the world" talk seems very much like a marketing ploy/propaganda to me. Even when I was in China, the only people driving Chinese EVs were those from the middle to lower socio-economic class people. Chinese with money drive European cars.

text:
Chinese car companies are inflating their sales numbers. They "sell" new cars to subsidiary shell companies that just store them in giant parking lots (and eventually sell some of them on the used market). They get subsidies from the government for every vehicle they sell. They also show investors the giant sales numbers to get even more investments. It is an unsustainable  ponzi scheme that is very bad for the environment and gullible investors.

text:
1:52 - the 334 miles displayed on the Tesla “charging” screen here is NOT what the trip computer thinks the car can do. It's simply the rated (US EPA) consumption x percentage of battery filled. It is not accounting for the current weather, the route entered, or your personal driving style. It's essentially just an alternate way of displaying the state of charge percentage (which is why it's in the charging menu, not in the trip planner or the consumption info).

What you should have been looking at is the estimate given in the Consumption app.

I doubt this was done to intentionally be deceiving, but it just shows why EVs need to be reviewed by people that are familiar with the systems on board.

I suspect the same mistake might have been made on some of the other cars in this video too.
clean_text: 1:52 - the 334 miles displayed on the tesla “charging” screen here is not what the trip computer thinks the car can do. it's simply the rated (us epa) consumption x percentage of battery filled. it is not accounting for the current weather, the route entered, or your personal driving style. it's essentially just an alternate way of displaying the state of charge percentage (which is why it's in the charging menu, not in the trip planner or the consumption info). what you should have been looking at is the estimate given in the consumption app. i doubt this was done to intentionally be deceiving, but it just shows why evs need to be reviewed by people that are familiar with the systems on board. i suspect the same mistake might have been made on some of the other cars in this video too.

text: The problem with your range tests is that you are talking about the claimed range which is based on the WLTP driving cycle. And you are driving a lot on the motorway in high speed. No manufacturer has ever promised that their car would go the claimed WLTP range mainly during motorway driving. Thats not the case for ICE cars either. So the results are quite impressive but it sounds like a disappointment if a car get below the claimed range. Also, the WLTP figures are always for driving in 23 degrees outside temperature. If you would make a test where you would try to mimic the WLTP driving cycle, that would be more accurate and more interesting.

text:
The Audi should approximately  have been given the same amount of energy in the tank as the electric cars then this test is fair. 

Tesla 75kWh, Kia 78kWh, Skoda 77kWh, Xpeng 84 kWh, BYD 91 kWh and Leapmotor 70 kWh makes an average of 79 kWh which is 284 MJ(megajoules)
Depending on blend and quality 1 gallon of gasoline contains 131.7 MJ which would allow the petrol car 2.2(2.156) Gallons of patrol 
So if you allow the petrol car the same amount of energy as all the other cars in this test according to the average miles a gallon shown here 47:04 at 25.2 mpg the actual range of the petrol car in this test would be 2.2 x 25.2 = ~55.44 miles if you would drive the car completely empty.

text:
Even a giant Audi SUV that does crap mpg will outlast current EV's. If you're going to run these, shove a used 2 litre mk4 or mk5 mondeo diesel in there (or something similar). It'd have averaged 55+mpg, & would have had 1/2 to 2/3 of a tank left at the end of this. Just as practical as any of these, way cheaper to buy, cheaper to insure, cheaper to repair, cheaper tyres. All of which will easily offset any of the costs of home charging. The CO2 deficit of running an old diesel will still be better until those EV's have some serious mileage under their belt, especially if they're charged on the normal electric network running mainly on fossil fuels. Also, proper buttons in all of them for HVAC etc.

I'd have an EV, but it would have to be used, & it would have to be a practical hatchback or estate with a decent range, & be able to be maintained by an indepedent garage. The technology & the market are nowhere near there yet, & I'm not sure they ever will be as it stands. New EV's are for wealthy people, or people who don't mind being in massive debt, just for a car. I'll carry on owning my shitbox outright thanks.

Good video though.

text:
These tests are ridiculous. Comparing EVs under their worst case driving environment to an IC car under its best case driving environment. And then coming up with a cost comparison based on buying electricity at the highest possible price. No mention of the difference in efficiency…..25 mpg versus well over 100 mpg. Carbon emissions are apparently irrelevant to this comparison.

First, how about a real world range comparison? Maybe 40% motorway and 60% city? I'll bet the results would be quite different. Next, why is a 325 mile range problematic when a great many performance IC vehicles…such as the BMW M2 won't get that? 

Next, this kind of testing is more or less irrelevant even to real world motorway driving. As someone who has owned a large number of IC vehicles, and has switched over to EVs 6 years ago, I find that none of this matters. I typically drive no more than 3 hours at a time, generally closer to 2 hours on 900 mile journeys. This is no different than with my IC vehicles. Charging the car at rest stops always happens more quickly than it takes for me to do what I need to do at rest stops.

What's going on here in the suggestion that the one thing that some IC vehicles do relatively well is the most relevant consideration…when it's not. 

Most folks who operate EVs will charge at home, in many cases for free. EV energy efficiency is about 4x that of a comparable IC vehicles. Cost of maintenance is far lower with an EV than with an IC vehicle. Most importantly, EVs are far better for our rapidly deteriorating environment.

While it's great to be a wealthy car sales guy with a charming personality, perhaps continuing to put out sensationalist content that can only harm the necessary transition away from vehicles that waste 85% of the energy they use is not terribly responsible.

text:
This test makes absolutely no sense. Sure, you could have a massive fuel tank in your car and drive thousands of miles without refueling—but petrol is far too dangerous for that to be legal! By that logic, why did I ever put my kids on top of a petrol tank built under the floor in the back of my car? I just never thought about it as thats how it was (still is) done. As a kid, we didn't use seat belts either—that was also pretty stupid.
When you buy a car, you don't ask how far it can go on a single tank—you ask how many miles per gallon it gets. That's the real point. Efficiency—miles per watt in an EV—is far more important than total range on a single charge.
I've had my Tesla for 8 years and have driven over 88,000 miles. Happy to take questions from anyone who wants to know what it's really like using an EV every day.

text:
I have 3 EVs and 1 gas vehicle. The gas vehicle is there for holding my flame thrower fuel. Lot easier to jack down a battery pack and repair modules or even replace it than it is to hoist an engine out and drop in a crate. I've done both. I enjoyed my time melting tires in gas cars but gas cars have reach point of diminishing returns.

As for the fire risk - the word you're looking for is incidence. It's the total occurrences during a given time period divided by total possibilities. So while there are many gas car, there are a higher ratio of gas car fires than EV fire ratio. Actuaries look at those numbers when modeling risk rates. It varies by model, but EVs generally catch on fire thus far at about 1/5th the rate combustion cars do. When they do ignite it is much more difficult to put EV fires out due to higher burn temps and self fueling (batteries are their own oxider, fuel and heat source). If you live close to a fire station this might matter since it could mean the difference between part of your home vs all of your home burning. If you're not in a 2 min response window your home is toast no matter if it was a horse that kicked over a kerosene lamp in the barn or an EV fire. Most people don't assess risk properly, otherwise they would actually bother to check their tire pressures instead of worrying about fires beforehand.

text:
The difference between gas cars catching on fire and electric cars, is that electric cars catch fire when they're not being used in your garage, driveway, parked, etc. 
A gas car won't catch fire if it's been sitting there overnight. It takes heat to ignite the gas. Electric batteries are constantly holding back A LOT of power and once the battery has a fault, it ruptures. Can even be explosive with no warning at all. 

Electric fires are a lot more violent than gas and take like 10x the amount of time to extinguish.

How to keep a battery good for as long as possible:
Don't charge passed 80%
Don't drain passed 20%
Don't fast charge
Don't use while charging
Don't expose to extreme temperatures
Don't subject battery to impacts

text:
My best friend is a HUGE advocate for EVs. *But* he has a working brain. He bought a Chevy EV that is 3 years used. He installed a charging station in his garage, at his HOUSE. With the help of his Master Electrician father. He only uses it as a commuter vehicle going just a few miles to and from work and home. He plugs it in every night. He has no plans to keep it long-term before trading it in. 

He has a wife. She drives a brand new leased internal combustion vehicle (usually an SUV) every couple of years. One guess which vehicle they take on road trips. Yeah, NOT the EV with its limited range. Few months back, they drive across two states to attend a wedding. The Chevy stayed home! They took turns driving her gasoline powered Buick. Mostly because charging stations are not even remotely as common as gasoline stations. BTW, when the lithium batteries inside EVs catch on fire, the fumes they give off are literally toxic! You breath those in, you reck your lungs. Oh, and no; your lungs don't recover. You get severe respiratory issues for the rest of your life. That doesn't happen when gasoline powered vehicles catch on fire.

text: I leased a new 2018 Nissan LEAF S and tried that 120 volt extension cord trick, but only 25 feet to reach from the garage. The cord got very  hot, and the owners manual warned not to use ANY extension cords. My LEAF also had a 50 amp 240 volt plug and I installed a NEMA 14-50 outlet to charge SAFELY in about 5 hours. It didn't really matter because the range dropped in half when the temperature was 20 degrees. That's right, the 150 mile range turned into 75 miles and became useless driving anywhere out of town. My friendly Nissan dealer blamed me for "operating the vehicle improperly". Fortunately, Carmax bought it after only 3 weeks. I lost $7,500 but I learned a painful lesson and got rid of a car I could only drive in moderately warm weather.

text:
@Hayden Schreier, I agree that 25% loss of original price within 1 year and 12000 miles is a lot, but I have to correct you: The depreciation of cars is usually not linear, but degressive. If you loose 25 % of the original price within a year and 12000 miles you do not loose another 25% of the original price within the next year and 12000 miles. The exact function surely depends on the car, but in general I think you could estimate that when it looses 25% of the original price within in a year, then it will loose 25% of that used car price within another year and so on. So over the years the price for a car will usually go down near to the material price of the components it consists of (which will depend on how well you take car of the car and if you do maintainance repairs). Maybe it's a different store for EVs, maybe their price will go down way below the material price of the components over the years, but the value of a car will never reach exact zero.

But I had to laugh when I saw that part where the one guy says that a new battery cell (of ten in total) for the Mustang EV costs 7000 USD and that it would be way less than a new engine on a combustion engine car. That guy is really delusional. In 2026, 7000 USD is almost the same price as a complete overhaul of engine and automatic transmission (with the work required to take them out and back in) of an 1998 Mercedes A-Class (W168). Actually, getting an engine and transmission overhaul on the 90s car is cheaper, because the 7000 USD of the battery pack does not include the price for removing it and putting the new part in.

text:
16:04 Steer-by-wire built by the lowest bidder is a recipe for serious injury.
True, aircraft have been using fly by wire for decades now, but aircraft have strict FAA regulations to follow.  Even the most minor corrosion calls for replacement of the part or service.  There are routine checks that happen on strict service intervals.  The aircraft is stripped down, inspected, and serviced.  What's more, each part needs to meet a certain exacting specification.  Be it torque yield, material composition, or physical dimensions.  Certain parts have substitutions, but those are non-structural or mechanical.  An aircraft will be grounded for irregular wear on a stabilizer bushing.

Until we have this level of scrutiny for vehicles, steer-by-wire is a disaster waiting to happen.

text:
I bring a tale of two sides:

I have a couple coworkers with Teslas, and they LOVE them. The one I'll tell about does have a house with a place to charge it, they live about 20min from work, and they did use it to road trip to the Alabama coast (we live in the Midwest) last year. They seemed fine with the charging break, said they'd do it again. A thing to note is this person is also a bit Type A and is prepared for most anything, so they most likely planned for their car ahead of time and it went very well for them. They overall had a blast on their getaway!! Something I do also like about this coworker, is they offer to carpool people to social lunch outings so that the rest of us who pay for gas can save a couple trips here and there.

My mom has a Wrangler EV. We call it a lemon, but, my state as absurdly lenient lemon laws so it doesn't qualify as one. It's in the shop every 1-3 months and I think it's a MY 2022. Because Jeep fumbled so badly trying to just get in the EV market at any cost, customers will come back with EV issues and the techs/mechanics don't know how to fix them because Jeep themselves doesn't even know how to fix them. 
One of the loaner cars was the Wagoneer EV and it refused to turn off, it was locked up so it couldn't roll, even after looking up and trying common ways to fix the issue. It was fully charged when this happened, so it's not like the ol' "let it die then turn it back on" thing was an option. After struggling to find a service to help, because the dealership was closed and unreachable, she had to call 3 different tow trucks before one who had the equipment to remove it showed up at like 3am to finally take it away. We were dead set on getting rid of it because it was in the garage and we didn't want to burn up in our sleep.
The other loaner was the Dodge, and oh my god that thunder noise on startup is SO corny 💀I would much rather it be a spaceship sound than have the overall impression of the car seem like a knockoff. Do they think people don't know any better?

text: 23:40 Actually vehicles with keys you turn and a very short-range immobilizer chip on the key are harder to steal than modern keyfob vehicles that transmit their key the whole time. Yes, it's encrypted, but that's why thieves have signal repeaters that don't need to decode it. Just one guy walking close enough behind you and the other guy is at your car, and they'll open it up, start it, and they don't need your key any more. It won't shut off for not having the key, because then if your fob battery dies while you're on the road you would be stranded. They can program in a new fob once they have the car. Yet another modern car safety feature that makes you less safe!

text: Regarding the catching on fire thing. The difference between a gas car and a battery car is obvious. An electric car doesn't need air to operate and for it to go 500 miles, has to carry all the energy it would take to go that distance. A gasoline car carries only part of the energy because it burns oxygen found in air. Remove of air and a fire will go out. A full tank of gas cannot wholly be lit on fire, you would need to spread it out and let all the fuel get exposed to air to get it to burn. So while a gas car may catch on fire, those fires can be extinguished and the fires are usually significantly less violent and more controllable.

We lost 20% of our battery after 10 years. We do drive carefully, but they dont deplete like you are saying.

No, gas cars don't spontaneously combust overnight.  All gas car fires happen while the vehicle is in operation.  Most are caused by poor maintenance. Motorsports probably sees the most gas fires, but slamming into a barrier at 120mph will do that.

I recently got a PHEV. It works perfect for me because I might drive 30km a day local. I get 170 km real world driving on the battery and have about 880km on the petrol engine. 

Because I drive mostly local, I drive in EV priority and I haven't used any fuel  in the last 3 weeks I've owned it and I charge it maybe 2 times a week using my stage 1 charger and costs me between $7-$14 per charge depending on how low the battery gets.

text:
My 97 Jeep Grand Cherokee gets about 250mi of range on a full tank(19gl) but i do drive hard. When the car was brand new, it probably got closer to 300+ mi in range. 
ICE cars, especially the older stuff, only really lost efficiency because of lost power from the wearing drivetrain- either from poor maintenance or just LOTS of miles of use. And even in those scenarios, most engines can still run good enough to get you around. EVs on the hand are literally dying just from merely existing. Batteries degrade from charge/discharge cycles, of course, but also age. Even if you bought an EV and never/rarely drove it, the battery will still not be what it is now 5 years down the road, and as that battery dies, the lifespan and output of the entire car dies with it.
Watch any "Will it run" video out there- engines that have sat for decades starting again once given fresh fuel, new oil, and a little encouragement. All those videos, the guys have a brand new battery to use, because that will always be a guaranteed item to replace cause age killed the last one, but the engine still had some life to give.

text:
It's pretty simple - I'm not as pessimistic on EVs but I do very much agree most automakers are making a lot of mistakes in the EV market.
My view is, as someone who enjoys my 2019 Model 3 Performance (which I have modified to an extent to improve efficiency, handling, feedback, etc + upgrading that boring and meh material steering wheel, lol) as a driving enthusiast and uses it for Amazon Flex work which I enjoy and also consider a hobby (where I can do well over 100 miles in a single day where my commute to the depot is 30 miles before I even start):
If you cannot charge overnight at home (especially on a cheaper rate), then it's not worth the money or the hassle. If you can, it'll be far more convenient and cheaper between the running costs, the maintenance being far lower and cars like the Tesla Model 3 Performance and Long Range having warranty on the battery (whether it drops below 70% BH or stops working in this time - free replacement) and drive train for the first 8 years and 120k miles (and the first 4 years and 50k miles having basic warranty which makes all maintenance free with Tesla till that's up too). Pretty much never have to use the public chargers beyond the rare long trips as I get over 200 miles on 80% which is more than enough for me as an amazon flex delivery driver (can do well over 100 miles which is more than most for a single day). And if I do have to on longer trips, the Tesla Super Charger network (at least when owning a Tesla) is a dream. Just find one, plug it in, automatically connects and charges just like at home. All paid automatically at the rate through your Tesla app and works out (at least in the UK) as cheap as a small little hatchback in costs (vs nearly x5-8 or so more efficient than the average petrol car per mile when charging at cheap overnight rates). As for the public chargers, at least in the UK, they're usually never working and super overpriced - wouldn't touch them for the life of me.
(Could also go on about how I really enjoy the instant full power on the street for limited-time-opportunities such as overtakes, roundabouts and junctions and the regen braking being a really nice thing but that's beyond the point of this comment.)
But don't get me wrong, other EV makers are far worse and ESPECIALLY EUROPEAN BRANDS are stuck in the past and many of them are far heavier for the same size of car which makes it far less enjoyable, capable in corners and less efficient and they typically have had higher starting prices. China's EVs seem to also be overly heavy despite being on the cheaper side while companies like BYD seem to be like ticking-time-bombs to go up in fire. And then there's companies like Dodge who cannot get basic electronic functions right and yet some people are surprised when they buy their EVs and find the throttle doesn't work correctly 😂.
So personally, I have no interest in touching any of the other manufactures yet. If Tesla wasn't around, I honestly wonder if EVs would've just died out before they ever got started.

P.S: In regards to the battery life, at least for Teslas, the average is around 90% IIRC for 100k miles. Seen many with 300k+ miles (where most ICE cars are scrapped at that point) with 75-80%+ remaining. And if you do need the 100% of the battery for a long journey, by all means, charge it up to 100%. It's there because it's meant to be used - just plan ahead. I bought an older Model 3 Performance for 30% or so of its new price around 79k miles because these cars do devalue like hell - getting it for such a massive discount after the depreciation is awesome. Just sucks if you like buying them new (but I'd never buy a new car, not worth it).

*TL:DR - EVs are only SUPER CHEAP AND CONVENIENT TO RUN if you can charge overnight from home in your driveway. If you have to rely on the public charger network, do yourself a favour and don't get an EV - it's not worth it even if you absolutely love EVs.*

text:
Okay ,I agree with many comments but disagree about the first gen Nissan leaf. They were probably the single most reason for EV adoption and they were dead reliable. The battery degradation issue is a minus and a plus. 
The first gen leaf did not catch on fire. The systems and software had multiple redundancy factors built in to prevent that. 
The leaf would go into limp mode or just flat out quit working.
I was a former mechanic and electrical design professional. I have had my own solar company since 1990.

The plus about the Leaf isthat they were engineered and designed to allow easy repair and maintenance.  The battery pack is safer because there is no problem with moisture intrusion or accelerated corrosion like vehicles with liquid or refrigerated heating and cooling. The leaf battery is a simple matter of disconnecting some plugs and dropping the battery out the bottom without affecting the rest of the vehicle. You can just swap out a new pack or open up the existing one and replace the bad module and put it back together.  Your not doing that with a Tesla because the battery is part of the structire of the car and the entire pack has to be replaced as well as complications with the active heating cooling system. Waaaayyy more expensive to fix or just throw the whole car away.

text: We've have two EVs for quite a while now and yes we have a level 2 charger at home.  These cars are the future we would never go back to a hybrid or straight gas car.  Gone on several long trips and no range anxiety or problems finding chargers.  Both EVs charge to 80% at a DC fast charger in 30 minutes.  Just enough time to have a bathroom and food break.  Cold weather does affect the range on the cars but still enough range to commute to work and drive locally.  We did a 1200 mile drive in late November and had no problems.  The only time I would recommend a hybrid is if you can't charge at home.  I just want to reiterate we've never had a problem finding a charging station on a trip.  If you're going on a trip it fine to charge to 100% because the real problem is charging to 100% and leaving the car sit.  Bottom line is EVs are great cars just be sure it has at least a 150 kw DC fast charge rate.  Buy used EV's because they are a great deal.  Our cars are both $60,000 new but we paid $29,000 and $21,000 for top of the line models and those prices are all fees and taxes included.  Both are 23 models one had 961 miles and the other 17,000 miles.

text:
I own a 2025 Cybertruck and a 2022 Tesla Model Y, and BOTH of those vehicles are simply amazing, fun to drive, fast, and most importantly, no maintenance  and I spend pennies a day to drive them. I've spent a total of $15 on my Model Y in the 3 years I've had it because I had to buy a cabin air filter!!....$15 in three years!! I've spent $0 in maintenance on my Cybertruck since I've had it and adults as well as children absolutely love it when they see it. My Model Y gets about 300 miles of range on a full charge that cost me about $3 in electricity at home. My Cybertruck gets around 325 miles of range on a full charge, and that will cost me $4 at most to charge it at home. So all together on a monthly basis, I spend maybe $40 a month to charge BOTH of my Tesla's....how much it cost to fill up that Denali again per week??..$80 - $90 per tank per week..oh ok!!

Want to talk about the battery going out in the Tesla...well Tesla has an 8 year 150,000 mile warranty on the battery, so I'm covered there....what's the warranty on that new Ford Expedition, 3 year 36k miles, oh ok!!

"But you can't take long road trips in an electric car"....WRONG!! Guess who has the number one charging network in the country??...Yes, Tesla does!! I can drive ANYWHERE in the country, coast to coast if I wanted to with absolutely no worries. The car itself will direct you to a Supercharging station which will be right off the exits on almost every highway!!

"But it takes forever to charge the Tesla on road trips"....WRONG!!! Most Tesla supercharging stations have 250 - 350kw stations, which means in 15 minutes, you are back on the highway resuming your trip.

By the way, how much does it cost you to change your oil, get a tune-up, replace your cat converter, have your antifreeze replaced, service your transmission, REPLACE your transmission, get your brakes replaced, get a new waterpump, replace the headgasket, oil leaks, New alternator, new radiator, emissions testing....and the list goes on and on and on.

But guess what...I don't need to get NONE of that crap replaced because and I dont worry about it because my vehicles don't have none of those things that WILL break....So whip out your calculator and and get your wallet and credit cards ready to be used and abused with that fine gasoline  vehicle you driving around in and I'll pray for you as much as I can. I'll see myself if I can recover from that massive $15 I spent two years ago on that cabin air filter I had to buy.

PS...If you buy an EV....BUY A TESLA PEOPLE!!!

text:
Before I even get through the video I have a few things I want to point out.

I want an EV. I believe looking to non combustible fuel options is a vital step forward for our society and our long term technological advancement. We should have adopted these options sooner.

That said, I will not buy a current EV because of how they are being built. 

We could have had EVs a lot sooner, there was an attempt to get them to market in the early 2000s in California. People wanted these vehicles, they mysteriously stopped being sold, there was controversy about it.

Back to the issues. Infrastructure is limited due to federal investment into building the necessary infrastructure being attacked at every turn. 

EVs are being built cheaply instead of robust modular circuitry they are being built with a central computer that runs everything. More coding means more potential problems. Nothing is being built for use on the road, they are being built to look good at first. Nothing that I will reasonably need to mess with on the road should be controlled by touchscreen. My car doesn't need network access. My car should not require connection to a corporate network in order to be functional (we will probably be seeing this in vehicles, including gas vehicles, in the next decade if trends continue).

Any issues that you see as being inconvenient to people around EV owners are primarily caused by lack of infrastructure not problems from the EV owners.

What we need more than additional private vehicles is robust, functional public transit. But we do need to also be working away from combustible fuel sources.

Li-ion batteries last the longest when they are between 20-80% SoC
LiFrPO4 is 10-90%

phone batteries are extreamly abused. They are often charged to 100% every night or every other night. Phones will have tramentious batterey degridation. EVs are far better. In fact they have been lasting longer than exspected. Limiting charge/heat, keeping within 50% is chemically most stable 

MAKE SURE YOU CHECK YOUR BATTERUES AIRFLOW/TEMPS!

text:
Based on current data, the percentage chance of a car fire is:
​Gasoline Cars: 1.5% (~1,500 fires per 100,000 sales)
​Electric Cars: 0.025% (~25 fires per 100,000 sales)
​Gas cars are roughly 60 times more likely to catch fire than electric vehicles. While EV fires are much rarer, they are harder to put out due to the nature of battery chemicals.

Some specific brands have faced higher fire-related recalls recently due to manufacturing defects:
​Hyundai & Kia: Often recalled for engine compartment leaks or electrical shorts.
​Ford: Recalled several SUV models for fuel leaks that could hit hot engine parts.

Do you feel safer yet?

text:
In my opinion, electric cars are only really suitable for people who own their own home, have a private charger, and solar panels on the roof. For others, they are far less practical.

Moreover, these vehicles are not well suited for long-distance travel because of the long charging times. Another important issue is that EVs are not truly independent once you have bought them. The manufacturer still has the ability to interfere with the car remotely, which raises concerns about control and privacy.

That said, I do see a future for electric vehicles in public transport, and especially for e-bikes. As for the environment, I believe the real solution is simply that we should drive less overall.

text:
Cell phone batteries and EV batteries are nowhere near the same thing, stop comparing them 1 to 1 like they're the same, they are not.

Modern EVs use Lithium IRON batteries whereas most phone batteries are based on lithium ion (or silicon carbide but that's newer and has nothing to do with battery health but capacity).

2nd point, EVs have active intense cooling and heating in them to make sure the battery does not fluctuate outside it's intended use temperature. Guess what most phones don't have and guess what? They all also are completely fine with the battery getting overworked cus that's not their MO. EV batteries are much more strict with battery temperature (most anyway, the leaf is ass and is only air cooled), the only allowing high temps when it knows you're going to charge soon since it will charge faster when hot.

5-7% loss in 5 years is normal but let's say you're right with the completely delusional claim of 25%+ capacity loss in that timeframe? Battery loss is not linear it tapers off. It goes down faster at first then slows to practically non-existence. It's not as if you lost 10% within 5 years that you'll lose another 20% within the next 10. It's more like you'll lose 4% within those next 20 years because that's how battery degredation actually works. You seriously need to do some research before you see all this stuff and comparing two completely different battery technologies.

Ps. Love your vids btw and still mostly agree with your take on electric cars, only get if you have at home charging and plan to keep it and run it into the ground. But your take on electric car batteries and long term reliability is absolute garbage and I have to call it out.

text:
EVs did not win because they were better. They were pushed into the market with subsidies, mandates, tax credits, and regulatory pressure. Governments have been propping this tech up for over 15 years because without incentives, most people would never buy one.
The environmental story is also heavily sanitized. Batteries require lithium, cobalt, nickel, and rare earth mining, much of it done overseas with poor environmental and labor standards. Those materials are shipped across the world on diesel powered cargo ships, hauled by diesel trucks, processed in coal heavy grids, then shipped again for assembly. Calling that “zero emission” is marketing, not science.
Once on the road, EVs are heavier, wear tires faster, and produce more particulate pollution from tires and brakes. When the battery degrades, replacement costs can exceed the value of the car. Recycling is limited, expensive, and nowhere near scaled to the volume being sold today.
Ownership costs are not cheap either. Insurance is higher, repairs are specialized, depreciation is brutal, and resale collapses once incentives disappear. Most buyers are locked into long loans or leases they would never accept on an ICE car.
EVs work for a small group of people with home charging, short commutes, and extra money. For everyone else, this was sold as a climate solution but functions more like a government-backed experiment funded by taxpayers and early adopters. The hype was loud. The math is quiet. Now people are finally doing it.

text:
The mantra: EVS are the future. Says who, I ask. Which of our overlords has decided this? Because the technology was never sound, the infrastructure is unfathomably unprepared for a full transition, we are only switching from one limited resource to another even more limited resource, and the market hasn't and won't adopt them without massive incentives. I'm not on any political team, but I am also not an idiot. The whole EV transition is bafflingly stupid and poorly planned, completely unsustainable, and it will cost us way more than we realize. It makes for a good fantasy. But I don't fall for fantasies and neither should anyone who reads this.

Also, the fact that these cars will need to be replaced as often as your phone, more than undoes any supposed environmental advantage they offer.

I cannot believe the world has gotten this stupid. Or rather, I wish it was surprising the world has gotten this stupid.

text: Preconditioning really takes care of the battery downsides in cold weather. Rivian has a "scheduled drive" feature where it can heat the battery before you leave, using energy from your charger at home, so you have the full battery range. Because the battery is so heavy, it will stay warm until you reach your charge stop, and charge quickly when you get there. I just drove from Illinois to California in an EV last week - we covered 800 miles in 14 hours, a lot of which was in below freezing temperatures as we crossed Kansas, Colorado and into Utah. I wouldn't have gone much, if any, faster in a gas car.

text: Where EVs use a lot of energy in the winter is when the car is parked on the street unplugged overnight and cold soaked. First few miles the efficiency will be in the toilet until the battery is able to heat up. After that, the battery will stay warm and the efficiency won't be that bad. On a road trip, the continuous driving, and the pre-conditioning when stopping at each supercharger will keep the battery warm. Supercharging will only take forever if the car is cold soaked overnight and then immediately driven to a fast charger without giving it a chance to heat itself up first.
