text:
3rd winter in Chicago with an Ioniq 5 and just cracked 80,000 miles in 2.5 years.  Yes, the range drops about 20-25%, it was about 35% drop on a -12º day, but that is all superceded by the fact you just plug in overnight.  Even with nearly triple the average US driving we have charged at a public charger 6 times total.    I did the math the other day and have saved 21 hours of pumping gas at the gas station (if you figure 5 minutes for going to the gas station, pumping, and getting back on the road, which I would say is probably conservative). 21 hours is a long time to be pumping gas. As long as you can charge at home, I don't ever see buying another gas car in our family.  My next vehicle is the Rivian R2.
In our Minivan that mileage would equate to 3,478 gallons, which is about half of a semi-tanker that fills the gas stations.  That is pretty crazy when you think about it in the bigger picture view than just the oh, I got 12 gallons of gas.

text:
I live in an apt building in NYC and bought a brand new 2024 Prius Prime back in 2024. I'm about to hit 30,000 miles and I must say, I'm so disappointed with the purchase.

My suspension is squeaking, plastic trim pieces are already coming loose and the range compete trash. I'm averaging 19-24mpg in the winter. Summer is better but not by much of using the AC. Without the AC, maybe mid to upper 30's.

The 1 year free charging from Charge Point and Toyota dealerships was discontinued 2 weeks after I purchased the vehicle.

Did I mention that 7 months into ownership, I had to jump start my car 7 times in one day due to a bad battery cell. I had to purchase a small junk starter pack that day just so that I could finish my daily routine. The better was replaced at the dealership under warranty of course, but it still took several hours out of my Saturday morning.


My GPS and Toyota app stopped working after a year, so no more Toyota GPS unless I want to pay a $15 monthly subscription. 
And contrary to what he said, my car takes 5-7 minutes to warm up to 74 F from cold start. Even longer in EV mode.

Also, I have a ton of scratches by the driver's door handle, which makes no sense considering I have no rings. What's even more odd is that my passenger side has very similar scratch marks, yet it has probably been used 10 times since I've owned it. I'm single, it makes no sense.

I was happy to buy my first Prius, one that finally looked good, but I'll be honest... I'll be happy to sell it, even at a small loss and just go back to driving my Honda Element.

Oh yeah, I drive mostly city, but travel 130 miles every week for snowboarding or mountain biking. Exclusively in Eco mode and have never actually driven in normal mode. I've tried sport mode a few times... The range drops very quickly. 

To be fair, I drove to Albany and back with another passenger, got nearly 60mpg. That was in the spring with no heat or AC use. And fully charged before the trip.

text:
One more thing: with a gas car, you can “take range” with you in the trunk or on the roof rack.

For example, my gas car allowed my wife and kids to cross the Slovakian border from Kharkiv, Ukraine (more than 1,200 km) in February 2022 without stopping at gas stations — just a full tank and three fuel cans I had prepared as a safety measure. Gas stations were also running out of fuel, but it could still be found here and there. They didn't have to spend time charging an EV every 200 km and were able to cross the country before traffic jams paralyzed all the roads. They also drove in a warm car and could sleep in it without any issues.

You might say, “This will never happen to me.” However, many people in my country thought the same — and paid for it.

Also, during blackouts you can drive a gas car as usual: gas stations run on generators. With an EV, however, you may not be able to charge it as usual. On the other hand, you could use the EV to power your house for some time 🙂

Therefore, I can only consider an EV as a second car, for example, as a city car.

text:
1. -14 °C isn't "that" much. When it's closer to -20, you start seeing a lot of EVs just randomly stranded. Also, even some Teslas apparently don't have enough heating power to keep the cabin warm enough for very cold temps.
2. The weight is what counts for the traction. Most EVs don't have some magical DSTC that gas cars can't have. You're still (primarily) controlling the traction via brakes and/or a locking diff.
3. That last point of just "getting in and going" is kinda useless. With most EFI cars, you just have to wait for the revs to go down before you can go, which often takes a maximum of 15 seconds, maybe 30 max if you were living in the Antarctica. If you were actually in a cold climate, you'd know you are shovelling your car out of the snow and ice during that time anyway. Or at the very least putting on your seatbelt.

I won't deny though that EVs are great for winters, especially when they can heat up so quickly. I'm just pointing out the potentially misleading info here.

text:
Maybe I missed it but I was surprised you didn't mention 2 things: 
1. Battery preconditioning for charging. We have an Ioniq5 and an EV9. It takes about 30 min but will enable the car to charge quickly at fast chargers. It's automatic if you put a charger as a destination in the car's nav system and you can turn it on manually. Really helps on road trips in winter. 
2. One of the things Snow mode does, at least in our vehicles, is lower the regenerative breaking levels which gives you much more stability when driving in snowy or icy conditions. I didn't know that on our first drive through a snowy mountain pass and it was not a fun drive with the whole family in the car. Winter tires also help :-)

text: Thank you for your video.  Very thought provoking.  I think personal driving habits and needs are a huge factor on choosing electric over gas or hybrid.  For me, each week is a "road trip" in that it involves a lot of driving.  Time and refueling places are a major factor for me.  My hybrid (gas and friction charged battery) is the best combination.  If power goes out (as it can in cold weather NE) I'd be dead in the water, looking for a free charging station.  And I don't have a clean garage to charge in, like a lot of folks.  That said, I predict better battery technology will make it interesting but the inconveniences of finding a charge in the middle of a trip and the time it takes to charge will keep me away from EV for a very long time.  Hybrid is a true win, for me.  I drive the 2021 Toyota Sienna.  Roomy and 500 mile range in all but winter when it drops to 389

text: I live in the Western NY area, and I'm on my 4th winter with my Hyundai Kona EV, I commute about 75 Miles a day round trip - after my first full winter with it I realized just how much the range drop doesn't really matter for the average commute. At 80% charge, even on a really cold day I typically have 135 miles of range, which is almost twice my commute. Even in a gas car you're still getting a range drop in cold weather, you just don't pay as close attention to it. text:
Good video to make. I have an ioniq 5 N which is the performance model with 640 hp and takes a big milage hit over the regular model and even more so in the winter. I have been driving since 1986 and cant see going back to an ice car for regular commuting.  Everything you said is spot on and having a garage is key. Most evs have preconditioning so although it take a small amount of planning its fine. So about 30 minutes from where i want to stop, i will activate it and the battery with be at the right temps for charging. I have done several 600-700 kms trips and i can charge from 10-80 percent is about 20 minutes. Isnit longer than pumping gas sure but im not in a road race so i go to the washroom. text:
My Tesla Model Y LR is my snow car simply because it is the only vehicle I own with AWD. I drive it to the ski resorts in Colorado on the regular during ski season (Nov through April - sometimes longer). Sometimes it will be well below zero F driving over high mountain passes (9-11k). For a frame of reference, in the summer, I can leave my home and reach Breckenridge with 35% charge starting at near 100%. In the winter, that drops to 25% and in extreme cases, about %15 upon arrival. Thankfully, there are several fast chargers around the area and I typically do a L2 charge while I'm skiing - so I'm back to my 80%+ for the return trip. It doesn't matter that much to me as long as I have enough energy to make it to my destination and chargers there.

It's just a different way to look at dealing with your car. For years, we just accepted oil changes and the standard stuff with gas cars. EVs are no different in that we just have to learn new gotchas or issues to work around.

text:
Worth noting that down to about -15C or so, batteries are more or less ok at least in newer cars. They get heated without too much energy loss from those temps.

It's when you go below -20C when things go off the cliff level of bad. Power significantly reduced and range goes down rapidly.

Had to do a less than 300km overnight trip in newest version of Y some time ago. Driving temperatures were just above -20C on the way back, and something like -25C on the way there. Car barely finished break in period, battery basically brand new.

It didn't make it without having to stop and charge at a station. And a significant chunk of "power bar" thingy on the screen was snowflaked until tens of minutes into the drive.

text:
Not only can you preheat in the garage, but you can schedule preheating/conditioning to occur every day, based on departure times. This was possible in my 2014 Leaf and in every EV and most PHEV's I've looked at or owned.

I take my daughter to the bus stop every morning before work, and in Minnesota it's been 0F or lower a lot of mornings this year, but I have it scheduled for 10 minutes after she gets picked up so it's warm when we take the car, when I leave for work, and even keeps the heat on if I turn it off while at the bus stop. It's all automatic, I don't even think of it. And if I'm plugged in when it first warms up, despite my charger being outside the garage, it has warmed up using the home power with little to no battery drain.

text:
We have  a Nissan Rogue and a Chevy Bolt, with 3 kids. And regardless of distance or temperature we just always take the Bolt without any discussion. It is a great car. 
It warms up MUCH faster than the gaz one. Never has trouble starting. like 99% of travels for most people are going to work and going to the grocery stores. For which the battery is more than enough even at -40 celcius. half the houses in our neighborhood have EV as well. And we all keep them outdoors all the time.
And even during long family road trips and even in very cold weather,  we have found that it changes very little, as we have kids and stop about every 200km for pee stops, diaper change, stretch our legs etc. (and charging time really isn't that long, by the time everyone goes to the bathroom and we buy coffee or some snack, it had already charged more than 50%)
And even if you don't have kids, stopping to stretch your legs every couple of hours is good for your health.
We have to change the Nissan due to 4th kid, and we are absolutely looking to get an EV.

Obviously, some people are not in a situation where that can easily work for them. Especially for those who don't have outside power (it is standard here for every house and every rental unit to have a dedicated outside power outlet, due to the cold requiring us to plug in our gaz cars otherwise they might not start) And yea, they are more expensive and there isn't a great used car market so not everyone can afford it. But if you can afford one, in most cases, it's going to be better than a gaz car.

text: Temps in the teens is not cold, that is walking the dog in a light jacket and grilling burgers kind of weather in MN. We have the Tesla model Y in MN. Yes, range is an issue in the winter but you plan around it. This winter it was consistently 10 to 20 below zero Fahrenheit, the range definitely took a hit. That was because we had to constantly precondition the battery and get the temps up to normal before we used the car. When taking multiple trips (home to the gym and back, home to the grocery store and back, etc....) and you have to precondition each time, it affects range. We've had to charge the car at home in the middle of the day several times. But that was the reality and we expected it. We have both a gas car and an EV, the gas car is the back-up vehicle. Sometimes, I would use the gas car so that the fuel would not gel up in the freezing cold.

text: Decent points made, but here in Sweden where all cars are designed for cold climate we have diesel cars with pre-heating, which don't need to be plugged in to heat. Sure it consumes diesel but that's still nice to be able to click a button from anywhere and warm the car and the motor same as you described your EV doing. One huge negative you didn't bring up is if you wind up in a stand still on a freeway for example. While it might not happen much, with some bad luck you could be stopped in minus degree weather for hours and in EVs you cannot use heating unless you want your battery to die. There has been people here freezing in slow moving traffic during snow storms because their EV has too low battery to both make it home and heat the car. Again, a rare problem for some. I would like an EV for short trips to town and during the summer, but I think I would choose my Ford diesel over an EV during the winter still.

text:
In the summer, my commute to work (70 km, mostly highway at 110–130 km/h) uses about 19–20% battery. In the winter, when it's really cold (-5 to -10°C), consumption roughly doubles. For example, it started at 90% this morning and is now at 50%, just enough for the drive home.

I love my EV for everyday use, it's incredibly easy, fast, and comfortable to drive. I was able to upgrade from a Toyota Aygo to a brand new Volvo XC40, almost from the savings on gas alone (fuel is icredibly expensive in Denmark).

 However, in the winter I do need to charge every day. Sure, I could drive slower and follow trucks like many people do on the highway to maximize range, but since I pay a flat rate for charging at home, I don't really think about optimizing efficiency.

Current EVs are excellent for daily driving, but they still don't fully meet every driver's needs.

text:
I mean.. I could've told you that over 5 years ago.

We had a local taxi company, cheapest fares in the city (Madison, WI), that had a fleet of Toyota Prius's for over 10+ years.

They got bought out by some rich Indian investor with some terrible green zero emissions company called Zeroology (terrible name too), they bought a fleet of Tesla model Y long range vehicles and replaced all of the Prius's, which was an incredibly dumb move.

We live in cold Wisconsin, so when winter came along, instead of 12 hours on a tank of gas in -10 F weather with a Prius, we now have Tesla's that barely last 3-4 hours in that same weather due to their horrible batteries.  The constant supercharging was nuts. They also installed super chargers and slow chargers all over the lot, which cost them $50,000+ a month in electricity costs thanks to MG&E having a local monopoly 😂 their plan was to build a big garage with solar panels, which would've cost even more.. it was overall such a dumb decision that completely ruined a company that was doing great and making a lot of drivers money.

We all lost our jobs after over a decade working at this place, and Uber and Lyft slowly took over. Now you hardly see any local taxi companies and see Ubers everywhere, which is just sad.

So another reason I despise Tesla, they took out my livelihood and I had to get another job.

text:
The interesting thing to me is - EVs hinge on you having a good place to charge them (apparently in a garage too which is very uncommon where I live - almost everyone parks outside cause garages are expensive), and then driving a moderate distance each day and then charging them again. Which I agree makes them make a lot of sense if that's all you do. Like he said, if you can afford one car for road trips and one car for other stuff that's ideal but again, many people can't. (heck, I'll say in an ideal world you'd have multiple special use vehicles like for towing). 

But the negatives really seem to be reaching. Plenty of gas cars have snow mode, and while maybe the engine output can't be changed as fast as an electric one, the AWD systems certainly can vary power etc in very fast ways. Subaru, Honda and others have very effective AWD in snow - like magical.

If you park outside like a lot of people do, you can pre-heat a gas car just fine. Even then, I bet if you ask many many people if they're OK with a car taking 6 minutes to blow hot air in the winter the answer will depend massively on the other tradeoffs / cost to do so. For decades we declined the hundreds of dollars to get remote start over running outside, starting the car, and then coming back inside while it heated up. Heck, lots of the time you have to go out there anyway to get the snow / ice off. And if you're in a garage, you're usually not heating from 7 but from at least 45-50, and you still have to potentially clear a path from the garage door to the road.

And as he stated, outside of performance cars, at least since the early aughts it's been unnecessary to "warm up" a gas car before setting off. People might like to because it takes them time to get going for whatever reason, or because of what they grew up with or whatever, but it's not necessary.

I think the long and short of it is - EVs calculation actually doesn't change that much whether it's winter or not.

text:
I always charge overnight and have all the range needed (and then some) for the next day.  Unless I'm planning on a really long trip - in which case I'll bump the overnight charge target to 100% instead of the usual 80% - I am never sucking for range from my EV (ID.4) and that instant heat, steering wheel warmer, etc. just makes cold weather easily handled.  Max loss has been almost 20% of range, but it's usually closer to 10-12% loss.
With an EV, you can preheat in a garage safely and it doesn't smell like a furnace coming out of my vehicle on a cold, wet day any longer . . . it just runs, as consistently and quietly as it always does.
And for longer road trips, the car's built-in navigation automatically recommends and adds charging stops on my trip, so I don't have to plan ahead if more range is needed. Plus, I really don't mind hooking up at a charging stall, then visiting the local store or restaurant to relax and recharge my own body.  It's a more comfortable way to take longer trips, really.

text: afaik most EVs only have one motor that spreads its power to all 4 wheels. which is literally the exact same thing as a gas engine if you consider traction control. And last time I checked, the best traction control awd systems were the ones manufactured by BWM, Mercedes and Audi - all of them semi- or fully mechanical systems that can distribute torque from all 4 wheels down to only 1 wheel in a matter of milliseconds. A (semi-) mechanical system will ALWAYS be better in this then any sensor-actor combination as it works through physics, which means it has no delay. Pullin a car out of a ditch has almost nothing to do with traction control, it has to do with maximum torque and tires.

text: I was totally in agreement with MKBHD right up until the end when he said "... you can just get in an EV and go" in cold weather. I learned the hard way this wasn't true in every case. In 2023 it was -11°F and I decided to just get to work fast. Dash lights warned me about the cold and the car went into "turtle mode" and SLOWLY accelerated barely reaching 55 with no additional capacity to accelerate and pass or maneuver. About the end of my 20 minute commute it finally turned off turtle mode as I pulled into the parking lot. So, no you just can't get in and go at the lowest temps! There's no engine and fluids to warm up, but the battery can't be cold soaked and expected to run!

text:
I only have an EV and it gets really cold here in Alberta.  NJ cold minus 20F.    Range is half but heat is fast, even street parking is fine if you can block heater plug in.  Level 1 at 8 amps works fine.  

Road trips are different in EVs.  You plan your trip stop for lunch or dinner breaks, while you charge.  Many chargers are close to or built in at restaurants.  

It is all about snow tires.  

Ironically highway driving uses less power than city streets in cold winters when you run the heater constantly.  At -30F you use 13 kW to drive at 62 mph and 7 kW for the heater.  At 31 mph you use 5 kW to drive and 7 for the heater.  One uses 20 kWh to go 62 miles the other uses 24 kWh to go the same 62 miles.

text:
I'll preface by saying I feel like mine is a fringe case: I own a 2016 Nissan Leaf and it's undrivable in the winter.

I bought it used (first time EV owner, super uneducated admittedly) while I was living in a place where I could charge it overnight in a garage. On a full charge it typically reads 78 miles, but obviously doesn't get that even without turning on the climate control. Turning on climate control absolutely cooks the battery, plus if it's under freezing it goes into "low power" mode which cuts the milage into a quarter of it's overall stated range. Also, I moved so I no longer have a way to charge it while at home.

So that said, the car just sits in a parking spot for the colder months. I love owning an EV, but am definitely looking forward to trading it in for something with a far superior range when I'm back in a place I can charge it overnight!

text: I have been hyped to get an EV but after my boss got one and dialing back the excitement for common sense I'm less hyped untill they get better.  I see the little compromises he has to do in order to have one.  If I consider filling my gas truck as "charging it".  I charge my truck every 2 weeks and don't even think about it in between.  I remote start from my house on cold days so it is warm when I get in and the heated seats and heated steering wheel are warm.  There are high speed chargers everywhere 10mins to fully charge (fill with gas).  I can at a moments notice decide to go on a long trip with half a charge and just fill up at several towns later and there is always a charger (gas station) available and it works.  All the chargers just use my normal bank card or credit card.  I can tow my camper across country whithout adding dramatic charging time to my trip.  I can go off road into the mountains and not worry about my charge level and could even take a portable charging (jerry can) with me.  The extra I pay for the charging fuel (gas) is a wash against the higher price of an equivalently capable EV.  The peace of mind having a gas vehicle seems better for me that all the compromises of an EV.  I am hopefull the battery tech and charging stations catch up and prices go down so I can reconsider for what seems like cool tech, maybe less maintenance, even more power but I have lots anyway.

text:
I live in Alberta, Canada and I don't recommend that anyone jump into their vehicle and drive away immediately when it is extremely cold outside. Your breathing could cause the windows to fog up really soon after you pull away, which could cause a dangerous situation. I am retired, so I only fill up about once per month in the winter, which is not an issue for me. In the winter, I like my vehicle to be nice and warm inside, and I enjoy my heated steering wheel and heated seats.

An ICE vehicle meets all of my needs, but I could also get along with an EV if the price was right.

text:
I drive a 24 Equinox EV, and its performance is cut in half during the coldest winter months. I'd say our average cold day in the winter is about -20 C / -4 F. However, we get at least 20-30 days of -30 C / -22F. I also drive a long distance for work. 171 KM / 106 M in distance round-trip. 

On the coldest days, driving 171 KM I charge to 100% and get home with about 10% left. The car's range is about 450 KM / 279 M on a full charge. Meaning, I drive only 171 KM, but because of the extreme temperature, it is as if I have driven 400 KM. 

Based on climate and distance traveled, I know this is an extreme case. Regardless, I do not regret the purchase. I would get the vehicle again.

text:
I have an electric. It doesn't dip that far below freezing where I am, but it does dip below freezing.

I have an old-ass electric. It is ten years old. It did not have huge range to start with. But it's still fully capable of getting me where I need to go, even when the battery is cold.

If I lived in a place where Fahrenheit and Celsius sometimes agree on how cold it is (like where I grew up), I might get a lot more interested in owning an electric there if the sodium battery formulations started showing up in EVs. Sodium batteries have lower base capacity, but are a lot less affected by the weather.

EVs seem like the unchallenged winner if you're normally doing in-city driving or otherwise not driving more in a way than you can easily charge at home where you get the cheapest price on electricity. Probably still better with a hybrid if you drive a lot, though.

text:
Electric cars work just fine in the winter.  Speaking from personal experience.  Heck they sell heavily in the Nordic countries like Norway where it definitely gets cold a lot.  Only real downside is you lose range, usually losing about 25%-33% of whatever your usual summer range is when it gets below freezing.  Otherwise they work great. Mine has excellent traction control, preheats easily, heats up fast, and I never have to freeze standing at a gas station.  Heats up just fine and I've got enough range that even with the cold weather losses long road trips happen without difficultly.  Use winter tires in areas where that is appropriate just like with a gas powered vehicle.

On the rare occasion where you might be significantly advantaged by driving a gas powered vehicle for a long trip, one can always rent a vehicle for the occasional road trip.  Most people won't need this though if their vehicle has over 300 miles of rated range.

text:
From a daily driver perspective, moving to an EV has a been a better experience for 98% of the miles I drive. Being able to silently warm up the interior prior to leaving at 6:30AM is such a nice treat for me and my neighbors. When I go to pull out on the high speed main road that's just 50 seconds from my house, I don't have to wait for a big gap in traffic because my engine oil is still very cold.

The ONLY advantages my old gas car had were overall range, refueling times and gobs of waste heat for when it gets really cold. In every other situation, it's a worse daily driver. It would be very hard for me to ever go back to a gas-only daily driver. If charging at home or work were no longer an option, I might begrudgingly downgrade to a hybrid.

text:
Okay lets assume what you say is correct.

But lets not forget
1) If they catch fire you can't put them out
2) they cost more
3) If you leave the out in very cold for multiple days they will brick and have to towed to a garage warm up
4) you cannot charge them off road or remotely
5) they are not suitable for long road trips because of the time to charge and lack of charger availability 
6) in an emergency (hurricane flood etc) you can't charge them
7) you need a garage (see 1)
8) they are really heavy
9) they take power from the grid that was generated by fossil fuels with 30% efficiency 
10) until they switch to solid state many condo's/buildings/ferries won't let them park (see1)
11) if you forget to charge them, you can't fill them up quickly
12) if your battery bricks after warranty period, the car is a write off, because the batteries go obsolete and are redicously expensive and the battery becomes more risky with time (see1, 10)

Buy an e-car, if you like the acceleration and the branding - or you commute from your garage to a underground parking and nothing else. Buy an energy efficient car (eg Honda civic) if you care about the environment. Buy a big ICE SUV if you are hauling your family and toys.

text:
People forget gas cars lose range in the winter too. Between warming up (for those who do that), the computer making the vehicle run richer in the cold, and winter blend fuel, you also end up losing MPG. In the winter my Tacoma will typically get 15-18MPG with highway (70 MPH) commuting while the summer will typically be 20+ MPG.

Also, EV traction also has a lot to do with the fact that they're significantly heavier which contributes. While pulling "larger" vehicles out like the F150 (Rivian is actually heavier) or the semi is impressive, it isn't as impressive as you think if you've ever watched Matt's Off Road Recovery.

text:
All true and great and applicable only if all you do is commute in the city and have a guaranteed place to charge. But some people have different needs out of their vehicles.

If you, for example, find out that NOW you need to depart to transport 500kg worth of products over 800 km during the winter... yeah, you ain't doing that with an EV. You do that with either gas or diesel. Remove the 500kg of products? Still not an EV thing. Remove the winter? Still not an EV thing.

EVs do have advantages, but they are pretty much limited in being useful only in cities, in a lot of countries chargers are pretty much impossible to find around the place, are slow, always busy, and cost more than gas. It's pointless, it's expensive, and it's annoying.
But what are you going to do, buy a car at a premium exclusively for city usage without ever envisioning using it for a road trip, moving, or literally any other thing you can only do with a car? Nah. It doesn't make sense.

This is the kind of consideration which, respectfully, you can only do when you don't mind having more than one car, one of which is dedicated for city usage, with a dedicated garage to charge it every night. And most people do not live in these conditions.

text: This is pretty spot on. We are a 2 car family 1 Ev and 1 plug in. The plug in is our long trip car. People need to get away from thinking in terms of road trip as being this crazy long trip. We have to drive to far games for our kids or to college, and easily a 4-6 round trip journey driving highway speeds in the winter sucks for an EV. Last thing anyone wants to do is have to stop to charge for 1/2 hr. They just want to get home. But otherwise everything else the EV is superior, every day wake up to a full charge. I think if 400 range is the norm then we are talking just about perfect. You can drive hwy in cold and do a round trip 5-6 hours. But really the solution is to have level two chargers available everywhere. Thus, if you were taking a three hour trip somewhere you can plug-in at arrival and then when you leave, you'll be fine. Unfortunately, this is not the case, I think my issue stems from the fact the destination never has the level 2 charger and if it does there are 2 stalls tucked away somewhere that are taken… and I am not taking random places I am talking colleges, schools, public fields, complex's.

text:
OK, here in the UK the average temp during winter is around 4 ○C. My EV range drops around 40 miles or so. 
Thing is, I work 48 weeks in the year, 47 miles to and 47 miles from home. 

I go on 4 journeys per year of around 530 miles. The first 220 miles are virtually free (circa £5) the remainder costs around £0.27  per mile thereonafter. Driving in the uk, you will average around 47 miles per hour, so three hours of driving gets you around 141 miles. Break for 10 minutes, charge whilst on your break and you will have enough for a further 200 miles. So two 10 min breaks gets you all the way. This is the very same way, I drive an ICE car. I do have two cars, but in 4 months or so, my 5 year old ICE car gets traded in for a new mini jcw electric with even less range than my EV6.  

Those who know Evs, know that bullshit is still bullshit.
